competitors.label: MDN Destructuring
competitors.selectionReason: MDN is the canonical reference for destructuring syntax, defaults, renaming, and nested extraction rules.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative syntax reference for destructuring, defaults, and nested patterns.
theirGaps: It is less interview-shaped on where destructuring breaks in production: missing API fields, nullish values, and misleading defaults.

realWorldUseCases: Object destructuring is concise, but production bugs usually show up around missing properties, nested undefined values, renaming, and misleading defaults.
actionableExamples: Destructuring in Function Parameters You can destructure objects directly inside function parameters. This makes your functions cleaner and self-documenting, especially when handling configuration objects or API responses.
followUpConfusion: Common Pitfalls - Variable names must match property names unless you rename them explicitly. - If you try to destructure null or undefined, it throws an error because they aren’t objects. - Always ensure the object exists before destructuring — or use default values to avoid runtime errors.

realWorldUseCases: MDN explains destructuring syntax thoroughly, but our page is more explicit about the failure modes that make this topic show up in production debugging.
actionableExamples: The canonical docs still include strong syntax examples, so the example criterion remains competitive.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct on the next interview confusion: missing nested values, nullish destructuring throws, and defaults that look safer than they are.

actionableExamples: Add one tiny nested API-response example later if we need more example separation over the docs.

competitors.winReason: MDN is the canonical destructuring reference, but our page is stronger for the interview prompt because it centers the real production pitfalls instead of only the syntax.
